headline: Self-attention alone, no recurrence, beats RNNs on translation and trains far faster.

eli5.analogy: a group reading where everyone glances at every other word at once
eli5.text: Instead of reading word by word, the model looks at all words simultaneously and decides which matter.

plain_summary: The Transformer replaces recurrent sequence models with self-attention: every token can directly look at every other token and decide what matters. This makes training much more parallelizable than RNNs and captures long-range relationships more cleanly. The paper proves the idea on machine translation, where it reaches strong BLEU scores with far less training cost than previous recurrent systems. Its deeper importance is architectural: it separates sequence modeling from step-by-step recurrence, creating the template that later language models scaled into today's LLMs.

glossary.plain: A way for the model to weigh which other words matter.

demos.title: Softmax temperature
demos.caption: Drag to sharpen/flatten.

stated: Evaluation focuses mainly on machine translation and parsing.
Sequence length still matters because attention compares tokens pairwise.
unstated: The paper does not yet show the large-scale language-modeling behavior that later made Transformers dominant.
Attention is powerful but computationally expensive at long context lengths.

implementation.feasibility: weekend prototype for a small model; serious scale requires significant compute
implementation.cheapest_validation: Implement a tiny encoder-decoder Transformer on a toy translation or copy task and compare training parallelism against a small RNN baseline.

report_plan.reader_goal: learn the field and understand why the architecture mattered

sections.title: The bottleneck before Transformers
sections.takeaway: RNNs processed tokens in order, which made long sequences slow and hard to parallelize.
sections.content: Before this paper, strong sequence models usually read text step by step. That matched language order, but it limited parallel training and made long-distance dependencies difficult. The Transformer asks a cleaner question: what if each token could directly attend to the rest of the sentence?

visuals.title: Old sequence habit vs. Transformer move

items.label: RNN-style
items.text: Read token 1, update state; read token 2, update state; keep stepping forward.

items.label: Transformer-style
items.text: Let all tokens compare with one another in parallel using attention.

items.label: Parallelism
items.caption: Training can use modern hardware more efficiently.

items.label: Distance
items.caption: Far-apart words can interact without many recurrent steps.

items.label: Template
items.caption: The architecture became the base pattern for later LLMs.

sections.title: The core contribution
sections.takeaway: Self-attention plus feed-forward blocks can replace recurrence for sequence transduction.
sections.content: The paper introduces the encoder-decoder Transformer: stacked attention layers, position-wise feed-forward networks, residual connections, layer normalization, and positional encodings. The bold claim is not that attention is useful; it is that attention can be the main computational primitive.

steps.label: Positional encoding
steps.caption: Order information is added because there is no recurrence.

steps.label: Multi-head attention
steps.caption: Tokens look at each other through several learned views.

steps.label: Feed-forward layer
steps.caption: Each position is transformed after mixing context.

sections.title: What multi-head attention buys you
sections.takeaway: Multiple attention heads let the model track different relationships at the same time.
sections.content: A single attention operation can focus on one pattern. Multi-head attention repeats the operation in different learned subspaces, so one head can track syntax while another tracks alignment or long-range reference.

cells.label: Global context
cells.caption: Any token can use information from any other token.

cells.label: Parallel training
cells.caption: Sequences do not have to be processed one step at a time.

sections.title: The algorithm in plain steps
sections.takeaway: Queries ask, keys advertise, values carry information.
sections.content: Self-attention works by turning each token into a query, key, and value. A token compares its query to other tokens' keys, uses softmax to form weights, and blends their values. This produces a context-aware token representation.

steps.label: Make Q, K, V
steps.caption: Each token gets query, key, and value vectors.

steps.label: Weighted sum
steps.caption: Values are blended according to those probabilities.

sections.title: What the results established
sections.takeaway: The paper showed better translation quality with much faster training, making the architecture hard to ignore.
sections.content: The Transformer reached 28.4 BLEU on WMT 2014 English-to-German and 41.8 BLEU on English-to-French while requiring substantially less training cost than prior strong systems. The key evidence is not just accuracy; it is accuracy plus trainability.

sections.title: What the paper did not yet prove
sections.takeaway: It proved a new sequence architecture, not the full modern LLM story.
sections.content: The paper is narrower than its historical impact. It focuses on translation and does not yet demonstrate instruction following, emergent scaling behavior, retrieval, agents, or long-context efficiency.
bullets: Evaluation focuses mainly on machine translation and parsing.
Sequence length still matters because attention compares tokens pairwise.
The paper does not yet show the large-scale language-modeling behavior that later made Transformers dominant.
Attention is powerful but computationally expensive at long context lengths.

sections.title: Why this became infrastructure
sections.takeaway: Transformers turned sequence modeling into a hardware-friendly scaling problem.
sections.content: The practical lesson was simple: architecture that trains efficiently at scale can reshape the whole field. Transformers became the default base for machine translation, language modeling, code models, vision-language systems, and many retrieval or recommendation systems.

items.title: GPT-3
items.caption: Decoder-only Transformers show powerful few-shot behavior.

sections.title: Three papers to read next
sections.takeaway: These show how Transformers became the backbone of modern NLP and how the field addressed their costs.

papers.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
papers.why: Shows how Transformer encoders became dominant for language understanding.

papers.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
papers.why: Shows the scaling path from Transformers to general-purpose language models.

papers.title: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention
papers.why: Explains the systems pressure created by quadratic attention cost.

sections.title: Check your understanding
sections.takeaway: Five questions that test the method, field context, and product implications.

questions.question: What does the Transformer remove compared with many earlier sequence models?
questions.explanation: The paper replaces recurrent sequence processing with attention-based blocks.

questions.question: Why does self-attention help with long-range dependencies?
choices: Any token can directly attend to any other token
It only reads the first sentence
questions.answer: Any token can directly attend to any other token
questions.explanation: Direct token-to-token comparison avoids passing information through many recurrent steps.

questions.question: What made the architecture commercially important later?
choices: It scaled well on parallel hardware
It removed the need for data
questions.answer: It scaled well on parallel hardware
questions.explanation: Efficient training at scale made Transformers a platform architecture.

questions.question: What product risk follows from quadratic attention cost?
questions.explanation: Attention cost grows with sequence length, so product context windows have cost and latency trade-offs.

questions.question: What famous phrase titles the paper?
questions.explanation: The title captures the paper's central architectural claim.
